content:
Saturday night I had four terminal tabs open and a download bar stuck at 61%, and for about an hour there I really believed I was taking my job back from the cloud.

6 min read
Just now

--

Let me back up to Friday.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Friday the US government told Anthropic to cut foreign access to Fable 5 and Mythos 5, and within a couple of hours Anthropic just turned them off. Everyone. Paying enterprise seats, randoms like me, their own engineers who happen to hold the wrong passport.
One directive and the model doing half my job was a login screen that wouldn’t let me in. I already wrote about that recall the day it landed. This is the part after, where I decided to be a grown-up about it and got humbled by a hard drive.
Because of course my timeline knew exactly what to say. By Saturday morning every guy with a six-figure follower count was repeating the same line: run it local, own your weights.
One big account, Alex Finn, called the shutdown a wakeup call and told everyone to start running models on their home GPUs so no government can ever pull the plug on you again. And honestly it landed. I’d been a renter this whole time, and Friday felt like an eviction notice slid under the door.
Then, like it was on a release schedule, Moonshot dropped Kimi K2.7 Code that same Friday. Open weights, up on Hugging Face, a coding model specifically, the kind of thing I’d point at a real repo.
Modified MIT license, which for someone like me basically means do whatever you like unless you somehow wake up with a hundred million users. And the numbers in the announcement were loud. Plus 21.8% over the last version on their code benchmark, plus 31.5% on another one, thirty percent fewer reasoning tokens.
I didn’t slow down enough to notice that every one of those benchmarks was Moonshot scoring Moonshot’s own homework. No SWE-bench Verified, no Terminal-Bench, nothing run by anyone outside the building yet. Saturday I wasn’t reading the fine print. I was downloading.

So. The thing about a one-trillion-parameter model.
It’s mixture-of-experts, which means only about 32 billion parameters fire on any given token. The run-it-yourself crowd loves to wave that 32B around like it’s the spec that counts. It isn’t.
The model reaches for different experts every single token and you can’t know which ahead of time, so the whole trillion has to be sitting in memory, ready, all the time. The question was never whether my GPU could chew through 32B. It was whether I had anywhere to put the entire thing.
The entire thing, at the int4 it ships in, is about 610 gigabytes.
There’s a smaller build, a 2-bit quant some absolute hero squeezed out, that drops it to roughly 340. That’s the floor. The smallest version of this model that still does anything worth doing, and it still wants north of 350 gigabytes of RAM and VRAM put together before it’ll answer a single question.
I have a 4090. Twenty-four gigs. Sixty-four gigs of system RAM in the tower, which felt like a lot when I built it. Add it up and I’m sitting at 88 against a floor of 350. Not close.
Not even in the same area code as close. The download I’d been so smug about was going to finish and then just lie there, a 340-gig paperweight teaching me to read system requirements.
Fine, I thought, what would it actually take. Down the hole I went, because that’s the kind of weekend it had become.
To run the small quant at a speed that doesn’t feel like an insult you want a workstation with 512 gigs of unified memory, or a server packed with enough RAM that it limps along at eight to twelve tokens a second with the CPU screaming, slow enough that you’ll switch windows and forget you asked it anything.
To run it the way the API runs it, quick and parallel and not painful, you’re looking at something like eight H200s in a node. Six figures of hardware, not a home lab so much as a small mortgage that needs its own air conditioning.

And the timing is the part that stings. RAM is the worst it’s been in a decade right now. DDR5 is going for something like four times what it cost in the summer of 2025.
I went to price two 64-gig kits just to half-fix my problem and they were four to six hundred dollars each, for sticks that were a hundred and fifty not that long ago.
The move everyone was shouting, buy your independence, costs more this month than it has in years, in the same week they were all shouting it.

You can guess how this went.
Sunday afternoon I let go of the trillion. Pulled a 30B through Ollama instead, something that fits on the 4090 without crying, and you know what, it’s fine. Good, even, for a pile of everyday stuff. But it’s not the model on the poster.
It’s a perfectly nice Honda and I’d cleared my whole weekend for the supercar in the press release. And the actual K2.7, the one I’d downloaded the marketing for? I did the one thing I had left. I pointed my editor at Moonshot’s API. Ninety-five cents per million tokens in, four dollars per million out, or nineteen bucks a month for their plan.
Sit with that for a second, because I didn’t until Monday.
I’d just signed up to rent tokens from a company. A cloud I don’t run, owned by a lab in Beijing, that can change its pricing or catch its own government’s version of Friday whenever the wind shifts. I burned a weekend and a fat chunk of my bandwidth cap to get away from exactly that, and the escape hatch dropped me into the same room with a different flag on the wall.
Here’s the thing I came away actually believing, once I’d closed the tabs and sat in how dumb I felt.
The open weights are not nothing, and that’s where the people sneering at all of this get it wrong. A file on my disk can’t be recalled by a memo. Friday could not happen to a model I’d already pulled down.
The State Department can lean on Anthropic because Anthropic has a switch to flip; nobody can reach across the internet into my second drive.
If you’re an air-gapped shop, or a hospital, or a company that already bought the GPU cluster for something else, this is real sovereignty and it matters. It’s a strange moment that four of the top five open-weight models going right now come out of Chinese labs (Qwen, Kimi, GLM, DeepSeek) while the American frontier sits behind a login a government can apparently close on a Friday.
But run it local, handed to a normal dev on a laptop budget like it’s a fix? That’s mostly cope. The honest version of local, for me, is that 30B on the 4090, and it’s a real downgrade I can hold in my two hands, not the trillion-param flagship I’m going to keep renting anyway.
Sovereignty was never going to mean me running the best model in the world out of my spare room. It meant accepting a worse one I own, or paying a new landlord for the good one. Those were the choices. They were always the only two.
What got me in the end wasn’t the 340 gigs. It was how fast you should own this turned into spend six hundred dollars on RAM during a shortage so you can feel in control, and how neatly that rhymes with every other scary headline whose real answer turned out to be a shopping list.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Anyway The file’s still on the second drive. I haven’t deleted it, and I notice I don’t really want to, like as long as it’s sitting there I own a trillion-parameter model, even though I am never once going to run it. Maybe that was the product the whole time. Not the model. Just the feeling that I could.
